title: Dread Events
description: Small, creeping escalations of wrongness — the incremental events that shift a scene from tense to terrible.

entries.result: A door that was open is now closed. No one was near it.
Something smells wrong — not identifiably bad, just wrong.
An animal outside goes silent mid-call and doesn't resume.
A candle or light source dims without a draft, then returns to normal.
One of the clocks in the building has stopped. The time it shows is significant.
A sound that was continuous — insects, rain, machinery — stops, and the silence is worse.
Something that was where it was placed is no longer there.
Footsteps in an adjacent room that stop when the party moves toward them.
The temperature drops in a localized area without explanation.
A window that was shut is open. The latch is undamaged.
An item is not where it was left. It is somewhere it could only be if someone moved it deliberately.
A voice at the edge of hearing that resolves, when attended to, into nothing.
An object falls with no apparent cause — off a flat surface, in still air.
A mirror reflects the room but the reflection is a moment behind the observers.
All the animals in an area have fled or gone still.
A sound from below — from the basement, the crawlspace, the floor — that stops exactly once.
Writing appears on a fogged window or dusty surface. It's a name. Possibly someone present.
One lamp, candle, or light remains lit when all others are extinguished.
A door refuses to open, strains hard against the party, and then opens normally — with nothing behind it.
The sound of something being dragged, once, from the direction of the bedroom.
A chair is at a slightly different angle than it was.
One person in the group begins sweating despite the cold.
A child's toy is found in a building that has no children.
A painting or photograph has been turned to face the wall.
Blood is found in a small amount — a few drops, not a wound, no trail.
The water in a container has gone dark without explanation.
A pet — dog, cat — refuses to enter a room it previously had no problem with.
An insect behaves wrongly — one fly moving slowly in a cold room, a spider sitting still in the center of an open space.
Something taps from inside the wall. Three times. Then stops.
A food item has spoiled far faster than it should have.
The lights flicker once and return to normal. The moment of darkness was longer than a flicker.
A sound outside resolves into footsteps that stop directly outside the door.
One of the group's belongings has been moved to a different location — not hidden, just moved.
A locked room smells occupied.
An external door is found unlocked that was locked. No sign of tampering.
Something is wrong with the shadows in a specific corner — they don't shift when the light source moves.
A fire or heat source begins producing more smoke than it should.
A sound from upstairs: something heavy, placed down with deliberate care.
The radio or receiver picks up something on a frequency it isn't tuned to — briefly, clearly.
A full glass of water has been placed somewhere no one put it.
The fire goes out. The coals cool faster than they should.
A door that opened inward now opens outward.
Mud or dirt on the floor in the shape of footprints — going one direction, stopping.
A candle that was burning is found not burned-down but simply cold, as if never lit.
A plant in the building has died — not wilted, dead — overnight.
A book is open to a different page than it was. The page describes something relevant.
A noise from outside that is almost — not quite — a human voice.
All the clocks in the building have the same time. This is unusual because two of them were wrong.
A strong smell of ozone with no electrical source nearby.
One person reports hearing their name spoken. No one else heard it.
The kitchen is in order except that one cupboard is open, containing something it shouldn't.
A photograph is missing a person who was in it.
One of the group has a mark on their skin they don't remember getting. It looks old.
A bed has been slept in by someone, recently, while the building was supposedly empty.
Something has been eating the food in the pantry. Carefully. Selectively.
A locked cabinet is found open. Inside, one item has been removed. There is no key.
An old-fashioned telephone rings. The line is disconnected.
The door at the end of the hall is open. No one opened it. It wasn't open before.
Something has been drawn on the underside of a table — discovered only by chance.
A window that faces another building shows a room that doesn't appear to match that building's exterior.
A page of notes is missing. The handwriting for the surrounding pages is not quite the same.
A medicine or supply has been used — slightly, carefully — and returned.
One of the group begins laughing and isn't sure why and can't stop for a moment.
The building's front door won't lock from inside. The mechanism is undamaged.
A family portrait on the wall has been altered — not obviously, just the expression on one face.
There is ash in the fireplace despite no fire having been lit in what should be years.
A trail of mud leads from the door to the stairs and stops.
A noise like someone turning over in bed from the room above, which is empty.
The lights in one room don't work. The bulbs are fine. The wiring is fine.
A smell like rot — brief, intense, gone — with no source.
One window won't stay closed. Latched, it opens itself. Not from wind.
A door slams somewhere in the building. The building is empty.
The sound of footsteps on the roof. A single circuit, then silence.
One person cannot sleep. They don't know why. They feel watched.
At exactly the same time each night, something happens — a sound, a smell, a brief power failure.
A bible, diary, or personal journal is found open to a specific passage that is highlighted or underlined.
A chair has been moved close to a specific window, facing out, as if to watch something.
A list in a dead person's handwriting is found. The items on the list are household things. The last item is a name.
A mirror is broken in a way that looks deliberate — from the inside.
Something is behind the wallpaper. It is moving slowly.
The building's interior is colder than the night outside.
One of the group begins humming a song none of them knows and cannot explain where they heard it.
A scratching from inside a sealed room — rhythmic, slow, stopping when the party is still.
A phone message from days ago contains something in the background that wasn't there when it was recorded.
A piece of furniture has been moved to block a specific vent or passage.
All the food in the building smells slightly wrong, slightly sweet.
Tire tracks in the mud outside lead to the building and stop. There is no vehicle.
A child's voice from the garden, briefly, not repeating.
The attic hatch is slightly open. It was latched.
Something has been written in the condensation of the bathroom mirror, recently, from inside.
A piece of clothing belonging to someone long dead is found freshly worn — still warm.
A drawer full of keys with no labels, for locks that don't appear to exist in this building.
Every clock in the building stopped at the same time — a time that corresponds to a date of significance.
Handprints on a window, on the outside of a second-story window.
A room that was locked from the inside, that cannot have been locked from the inside, that is locked from the inside.
An obituary among papers for someone who, as far as the party knows, is still alive.
A faint sound of music from nowhere in the building — old music, tinny, as if from a record player.
A complete dinner prepared and set for several people, cold, in a building found otherwise empty.
The party's own names in handwriting not their own, on a piece of paper, in a building they've never visited.
Nothing happens. Nothing has happened. The group is leaving when one of them realizes: nothing happened at all. Not even the building settling. Not even wind. Nothing, for an entire night.
